exercises.title: Polynomial Ring Arithmetic
exercises.description: Implement basic polynomial ring operations for R_q = Z_q[X]/(X^n + 1).
tasks: Implement polynomial addition in R_q
Implement polynomial multiplication with reduction mod (X^n + 1)
Implement coefficient reduction mod q
Test with small parameters (n=8, q=17)
Verify that X^n = -1 in the ring

hints: When reducing, coefficients at position n+i subtract from position i
Use np.convolve for multiplication before reduction
Remember to reduce coefficients mod q after each operation

exercises.title: NTRU Key Relationship Verification
exercises.description: Verify the NTRU key relationship fG - gF = q for given key polynomials.
tasks: Given (f, g, F, G), verify fG - gF = q
Verify that h = g·f⁻¹ produces valid public key
Check that (f, g) is indeed a short vector
Compute norms of secret key polynomials
Compare with random polynomials of same degree

exercises.title: Gram-Schmidt Orthogonalization
exercises.description: Implement Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization and use it to measure basis quality.
tasks: Implement Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization
Compute Gram-Schmidt norms for a basis
Compare good vs bad basis quality
Explain relationship to signing efficiency
Calculate maximum Gram-Schmidt norm for given parameters
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def gram_schmidt(basis):
    """
    Compute Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization of a basis.
    
    Args:
        basis: List of basis vectors (numpy arrays)
    
    Returns:
        List of orthogonalized vectors
    """
    n = len(basis)
    ortho = []
    
    for i in range(n):
        v = basis[i].copy().astype(float)
        
        for j in range(i):
            # Subtract projection onto previous orthogonal vectors
            proj = np.dot(v, ortho[j]) / np.dot(ortho[j], ortho[j])
            v = v - proj * ortho[j]
        
        ortho.append(v)
    
    return ortho

def gs_norms(ortho_basis):
    """
    Compute norms of Gram-Schmidt vectors.
    """
    return [np.linalg.norm(v) for v in ortho_basis]

# Example: Compare good vs bad basis
# Good basis (nearly orthogonal)
good_basis = [
    np.array([1, 0, 0, 0]),
    np.array([0, 1, 0, 0]),
    np.array([0, 0, 1, 0]),
    np.array([0, 0, 0, 1])
]

# Bad basis (highly correlated)
bad_basis = [
    np.array([1, 0, 0, 0]),
    np.array([1, 1, 0, 0]),
    np.array([1, 1, 1, 0]),
    np.array([1, 1, 1, 1])
]

print("Good basis GS norms:", gs_norms(gram_schmidt(good_basis)))
print("Bad basis GS norms:", gs_norms(gram_schmidt(bad_basis)))
exercises.expectedOutput: Gram-Schmidt implementation with basis quality comparison
hints: Good basis: GS norms are similar
Bad basis: GS norms decay rapidly
Max GS norm determines sampling quality

exercises.title: NTRU vs Module-LWE Comparison
exercises.description: Research and compare NTRU lattices with Module-LWE used in ML-DSA.
tasks: Describe the key generation process for both
Compare the public key structure
Identify the hard problem in each
Research best known attacks on each
Explain why NIST standardized both
exercises.expectedOutput: Research report comparing NTRU and Module-LWE
hints: NTRU: h = g/f, no explicit error term
Module-LWE: t = As + e, requires error term
Both reduce to hard lattice problems
NTRU is older (1996), LWE is newer (2005)
